cataloging_subject.label: An Apology for Lollard Doctrines
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English prose defence of Lollard positions beginning ‘Here are questiouns and ansueris putte þat are writun here aftir,’ preserved as one item in Trinity College Dublin MS 245 and first printed under James Henthorn Todd's supplied title in 1842. This subject excludes the other works in the MS 245 miscellany, Todd's introduction, notes, glossary, and errata, the University of Michigan OCR/TEI representation, and the repository XML as distinct physical, editorial, digital, and local layers.

catalog_summary.title: An Apology for Lollard Doctrines
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Conventionally dated c. 1400; attested in a manuscript copied in the first half of the fifteenth century; first printed 1842
catalog_summary.region: England; the sole witness's scribal language is most likely Huntingdonshire in the southern Central Midlands

notes: Todd states that this title has no authority from the manuscript; it is retained as the established editorial and scholarly title.

titles.value: An Apology for Lollard Doctrines, Attributed to Wicliffe
notes: ‘Attributed to Wicliffe’ records Todd's qualified historical attribution; it is not a statement of accepted authorship.

titles.value: Here are questiouns and ansueris putte þat are writun here aftir
notes: The TCD catalog and Jefferson use this opening to identify the otherwise untitled manuscript item.

agents.name: John Wycliffe
notes: d. 1384; retained only as the historically attributed author.

agents.name: James Henthorn Todd
notes: 1805–1869; editor of the 1842 Camden Society edition.

assertion.notes: The current TCD item record expressly identifies this manuscript item as not by Wycliffe; no other named author is established by the evidence reviewed.

What is confirmed is the history and rejection of the attribution, not Wycliffe's authorship. Todd said there was no decisive reason to attribute the work to Wycliffe, and the current TCD item description says ‘Not by Wycliffe.’

notes: Do not promote the enclosing TCD catalog title ‘John Wycliffe, tracts,’ the U-M ‘supposed author’ field, or older library catalog author headings into accepted work-level authorship.

scope.description: Proof sheets checked against the sole manuscript, with assistance from Richard Gibbings

assertion.notes: The OED conventionally dates citations from the work c. 1400; the first-half-fifteenth-century sole witness supplies a secure upper bound but not an exact composition year.

notes: No normalized interval is supplied because the approximation is conventional and the author's identity and exact historical occasion remain unresolved.

date_statements.display: First half of the fifteenth century

assertion.notes: Jefferson dates MS 245 to s. xv¹; the current TCD catalog gives the broader fifteenth-century range 1400–1499.

notes: This is the production date of the sole known witness, not an exact composition date.

assertion.notes: Todd's title page says ‘Now first printed,’ and Jefferson identifies his Camden Society volume as the edition from this manuscript.

notes: England is the cultural and linguistic setting of this Middle English Lollard work; no exact place of composition is established.

scope.description: Scribal language of the sole MS 245 witness

assertion.notes: Jefferson's traditional analysis places the scribe in the southern half of the Central Midlands and her Atlas analysis finds Huntingdonshire most likely, with occasional more northerly forms possibly belonging to the exemplar or scribe.

notes: This localizes the manuscript scribe, not necessarily the author or place of composition.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: The U-M header's generic ‘eng’ label does not distinguish the medieval work's Middle English from Todd's modern English editorial matter.

language_statements.label: Latin

scope.description: Short biblical, patristic, canon-law, and scholastic phrases and references embedded within the Middle English argument

notes: Latin is embedded and quoted, not a co-primary language of the work; Todd's extensive Latin apparatus belongs to the excluded edition layer.

form_statements.description: A continuous prose defence organized as objections or questions followed by first-person answers.

assertion.notes: The first-person speaker answers charges or propositions and repeatedly offers correction by Scripture; Todd's modern title interprets this posture as an apology.

genre_statements.label: Question-and-answer treatise
notes: This is an objection-and-response form, not a dramatic dialogue between separately identified speakers.

subject_statements.label: Religious poverty, orders, labor, and begging
Scripture, canon law, and patristic authority

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English defence of positions associated with early Lollardy. After a prologue in which the first-person speaker submits every claim to Scripture and correction, the treatise responds to objections concerning papal and clerical authority, indulgences, excommunication, preaching and priestly ministry, the Mass and sacraments, poverty, fasting and prayer, churches and images, simony and Antichrist, marriage and canon law, discipleship, vows, charms, religious labor, and begging. Its method repeatedly tests ecclesiastical practice against Scripture, patristic authorities, and canon law.

Todd deliberately presented Wycliffe's authorship as an open question: he found the work compatible with early Wycliffite thought but admitted that its occurrence among attributed Wycliffite tracts was the only positive evidence and that there was no decisive reason to assign it to Wycliffe. The current TCD item record now labels the exact incipit and folio range ‘Not by Wycliffe’; this record therefore catalogs the author as anonymous and preserves Wycliffe only as a disputed historical attribution.

The repository encoding has one prologue and 29 chapter divisions. Its chapter attributes run 1–26, 28, 19, 30: there is no encoded chapter 27, and the penultimate heading is printed ‘XIX’ where sequence suggests ‘XXIX.’ Because Todd acknowledges adding some marginal summaries and references, this record reports the encoded/printed divisions without treating every heading or numeral as an uncontested authorial structure.

extent.description: One prose work represented in the repository XML by one prologue plus 29 chapter DIV1 elements. The sequence contains a missing 27 and a likely duplicated/misprinted XIX before XXX. The 394 body NOTE1 elements are mostly short biblical, patristic, and canon-law references or summaries and are not counted as work divisions.

evidence.locator: HEADER; BODY prologue and chapters; TRAILER
evidence.summary: Supplies the encoded Middle English text, U-M identifier, source-edition statement, OCR/TEI production note, chapter headings, subject matter, and final ‘Amen, Amen.’

evidence.citation: XPath and structural inspection of repository acm9160.xml.
evidence.summary: Counts 30 body DIV1 elements (one prologue and 29 chapters), 394 body NOTE1 elements, and 272 PB milestones: 64 front, 113 body, and 95 back. Chapter N values are 1–26, 28, 19, 30. The text transcription covers Todd pp. 1–113; most introduction and back matter is represented only by page milestones.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item ACM9160.0001.001.
evidence.summary: Identifies the item, Todd's 1842 edition, Wycliffe as only a supposed author, Lollards as subject, and the current public-domain/No Copyright–United States provider statement.

evidence.citation: An Apology for Lollard Doctrines, Attributed to Wicliffe, ed. James Henthorn Todd (London: Camden Society, 1842).
evidence.locator: title page; Introduction pp. v–xxiii and lvi–lxiii; text pp. 1–113; Notes pp. 115–188; Glossary pp. 189–206; errata leaf
evidence.summary: Confirms the supplied title, qualified attribution, single-witness transcription, Todd's stated editorial practices, first publication, edition scope, work contents, and old foliation 164a–218a.

evidence.citation: Library of Trinity College Dublin, catalog record CALM00230, IE TCD MS 245, ‘John Wycliffe, tracts.’
evidence.locator: Manuscript tab; call number IE TCD MS 245; local identifier CALM00230; description of fols. 164–218
evidence.summary: The exact institutional record describes a fifteenth-century 219-folio vellum miscellany. It identifies the Apology by incipit at old fols. 164–218 and expressly says ‘Not by Wycliffe,’ while the enclosing record title remains the older collective label ‘John Wycliffe, tracts.’

evidence.citation: Judith Anne Jefferson, ‘An Edition of the Ten Commandments Commentary in BL Harley 2398 and the Related Version in Trinity College Dublin 245, York Minster XVI.L.12 and Harvard English 738’ (PhD thesis, University of Bristol, 1995).
evidence.locator: pp. xx–xxvii and lx–lxvii (PDF pp. 21–28 and 61–68)
evidence.summary: Supplies a scholarly codicological description of MS 245 (C.5.6), its first-half-fifteenth-century date, corrected foliation, single hand, decoration, provenance, and exact Apology locus at corrected fols. 163r–217r; localizes the witness's scribal dialect most probably to Huntingdonshire within the southern Central Midlands.

evidence.citation: Oxford English Dictionary, s.v. ‘angel, n.,’ sense II.2, citation ‘c1400 Apol. Loll.’
evidence.locator: sense II.2, Middle English citation from Apology for Lollard Doctrines p. 31
evidence.summary: Supports the conventional lexicographical dating c. 1400 used here as a qualified approximation, not as a securely bounded composition interval.
notes: The exact OED entry requires JavaScript or subscription access; the citation wording was independently visible in indexed scholarly excerpts.

open_questions.question: Can current scholarship identify a narrower composition date, place, historical occasion, or author for the treatise?
next_steps: Consult the 2024 Index of Middle English Prose Handlist XXV entry for MS 245 and recent specialist work on this exact treatise.
Keep Wycliffe as a disputed historical attribution unless new direct authorship evidence supersedes the TCD record.
notes: The current record's c. 1400 date is conventional; the first-half-fifteenth-century witness is the secure terminus ante quem represented here.

open_questions.question: Which chapter headings, marginal summaries, and anomalous numerals are original to MS 245, and which were supplied or normalized by Todd?
next_steps: Compare Todd pp. 1–113 directly with images of corrected fols. 163r–217r if TCD supplies a reproduction.
Do not silently repair the missing 27 or duplicated XIX in work-level structure before that comparison.

notes: Todd's London publication place belongs to the 1842 edition and is not used as a medieval place of composition.
Todd's old foliation 164a–218a and the current catalog's fols. 164–218 correspond to Jefferson's corrected fols. 163r–217r because old foliation skipped 133; they are not different witnesses.
The sole manuscript is a composite Lollard miscellany containing 27 items in Jefferson's modern grouping; Todd's older description counted 29. Neither count is the number of chapters in the Apology.
